news_summary: Today’s environmental progress highlights global cooperation on climate action, innovative carbon-capturing technology, sustainable urban initiatives, and Indigenous leadership in wildfire management.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées environnementales d'aujourd'hui mettent en avant la coopération mondiale en matière de lutte contre le changement climatique, les technologies innovantes de capture du carbone, les initiatives urbaines durables et le rôle moteur des populations autochtones dans la gestion des feux de forêt.
news_summary_es: Los avances medioambientales de hoy ponen de relieve la cooperación mundial en materia de acción climática, las tecnologías innovadoras de captura de carbono, las iniciativas urbanas sostenibles y el liderazgo de los pueblos indígenas en la gestión de los incendios forestales.

articles.title: In a rare show of global unity, countries adopt landmark climate ruling
articles.content:
About six years ago, law students at the University of the South Pacific convinced the government of the small island nation of Vanuatu to take the harms wrought by climate change all the way to the International Court of Justice, the world’s highest legal authority. Vanuatu, along with the students, waged a campaign to convince the court that climate change was a human rights issue and that countries have a legal duty to protect the planet for future generations. In 2025, the court sided with them unanimously. In a legally nonbinding advisory opinion, it ruled that the failure of countries to tackle climate change is a “wrongful act” and that other nations harmed by a warming planet may seek reparations.

Now, the effort has notched another win. On Wednesday, an overwhelming majority of countries in the United Nations voted to adopt a resolution backing the court’s ruling. The historic decision signals the political support behind the court’s finding that countries have a legal responsibility to address climate change, reduce its impact, and offer reparations to those it has harmed. More than 140 countries voted in favor of the resolution. Just eight — including the United States, Iran, Israel, Saudi Arabia, and Russia — voted against (28 countries abstained from the vote).

“This must be a turning point in accountability for damaging the climate,” said Vishal Prasad, director of the Pacific Islands Students Fighting Climate Change and one of the law students who campaigned to take the case to the International Court of Justice, or ICJ. “The journey of this idea from classrooms in the Pacific to The Hague and the United Nations gives us continued hope that when people organize, the world can be moved to act.”

The near-unanimous decision is a strong signal that multilateral cooperation on climate change has not completely unraveled. Over the past year, global unity on reducing greenhouse gas emissions has proven shaky. After Donald Trump’s administration announced it would withdraw from the Paris Agreement, the United States has actively opposed climate action. Last year, it derailed countries that were close to setting a carbon tax on the shipping industry, which is responsible for about 3 percent of the world’s carbon emissions. A deal to regulate the industry’s emissions now seems uncertain. The U.S. has also helped kill a cap on plastics production and berated the International Energy Agency into projecting future energy demand under a scenario that climate action will stall out.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

“The unity and clarity expressed by the vote was striking,” said Nikki Reisch, director of the Center for International Environmental Law’s climate and energy program. Reisch said the resolution puts “political weight behind legal norms” and will help translate the international court’s conclusions into practical action. “It will become another pillar and proof of political backing for action and accountability.”

The Trump administration also mounted a campaign to block the United Nations from adopting the landmark international court ruling. In February, the State Department sent a missive to all consulates and embassies noting that it “strongly opposed” the U.N. resolution and that its adoption “could pose a major threat to U.S. industry.” In remarks ahead of the vote, Tammy Bruce, a former conservative radio host and now deputy representative to the U.N. in New York, said that the resolution is “problematic” and that “the United States continues to have serious legal and policy concerns” about it.

“The resolution singles out certain groups for preferential treatment and makes alarmist political statements, such as the idea that climate change is an unprecedented challenge of civilizational proportions,” Bruce said. “Such hyperbolic statements are not appropriate in a resolution on an ICJ advisory opinion.”

Tammy Bruce, deputy representative of the United States to the U.N. in New York, said the resolution is “problematic” and “makes alarmist political statements.” John Lamparski / Getty Images

The resolution reiterates the International Court of Justice’s core findings and calls on countries to implement measures to keep global temperature rise to 1.5 degrees Celsius (2.7 degrees Fahrenheit) while transitioning away from fossil fuels. It also affirms that nations must fulfill their climate obligations and that those countries harmed by others’ inaction are entitled to seek redress. Finally, the resolution calls on the United Nations’ secretary-general to submit a report next year on ways to comply with the international court’s findings. The resolution, like most U.N. resolutions, is not legally binding; rather, it’s intended to signal political priorities or views.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

The U.N. vote comes as countries are cracking down on climate activism and litigation. In Aotearoa New Zealand, the government moved to amend climate laws to limit civil court proceedings against major greenhouse gas emitters for climate-related harm.

Māori climate advocate Mike Smith is among those whose cases could be affected. Recent reports have found that land theft and colonization have exacerbated the effects of climate change on the Indigenous Māori people, who are more likely to be affected by extreme weather events. Smith is currently pursuing high court proceedings against six of Aotearoa New Zealand’s largest greenhouse gas emitters, and he describes the U.N. vote as a “major shift,” arguing it reflects a changing understanding of climate change not just as environmental damage, but as something with legal consequences.

“We know as Māori that the islands are part of our journey across the Pacific that’s led us here to Aotearoa,” he said. “New Zealand has a responsibility to stand with Pacific countries like Vanuatu, Kiribati, Tonga, and Tokelau. Not just symbolically, but in supporting stronger legal and international action on climate harm.”

Although the U.N. vote is a victory for Indigenous activists from the Pacific and beyond, they believe that many countries still must be pushed to uphold their climate obligations.

“The law is clear that climate action cannot sit on the shelf, it must be turned into action,” Prasad said.

The Indigenous News Alliance contributed reporting to this story.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a historic and significant global political consensus supporting a landmark International Court of Justice ruling that recognizes climate change as a human rights issue and holds countries legally responsible for climate harm. This resolution adopted by over 140 countries at the United Nations signals broad political backing for climate accountability, reparations, and stronger climate action, representing a meaningful step toward addressing the climate crisis with legal and moral weight. The story includes detailed context about the ruling, the campaign by Pacific island students, and the implications for Indigenous peoples and international cooperation, making it a substantive and inspiring environmental development with wide-reaching impact.
articles.personality_title: United Nations backs historic climate ruling recognizing countries’ legal duties
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – About six years ago, law students from the University of the South Pacific helped the island nation of Vanuatu bring a climate change case to the International Court of Justice (ICJ). They argued that climate change is a human rights issue and that countries must protect the planet for future generations.

**What happened** – In 2025, the ICJ gave a unanimous advisory opinion saying countries’ failure to act on climate change is a wrongful act and that those harmed can seek compensation. Recently, over 140 countries at the United Nations voted to support this ruling in a resolution, while only eight countries opposed it.

**Impact** – This vote shows strong global political support for holding countries legally responsible for climate change. It marks a rare moment of unity after years of disagreement, especially from countries like the United States. The resolution encourages nations to limit global warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius and to help those harmed by climate change. Indigenous groups, such as the Māori in New Zealand, see this as a major step toward recognizing climate change as a legal issue, not just an environmental one.

**What's next step** – The United Nations secretary-general will report next year on how countries can follow the ICJ’s advice. The resolution, though not legally binding, aims to push governments to take stronger climate action and be accountable for harm caused by inaction.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, most countries at the United Nations have agreed that climate change is a legal responsibility and that those harmed deserve justice and support.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les Nations Unies soutiennent une décision historique sur la responsabilité climatique des pays
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Il y a environ six ans, des étudiants en droit de l’Université du Pacifique Sud ont aidé le pays insulaire de Vanuatu à porter une affaire climatique devant la Cour internationale de Justice (CIJ). Ils ont soutenu que le changement climatique est un problème de droits humains et que les pays doivent protéger la planète pour les générations futures.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2025, la CIJ a rendu un avis consultatif unanime disant que le manque d’action des pays face au changement climatique est une faute et que les victimes peuvent demander réparation. Récemment, plus de 140 pays aux Nations Unies ont voté pour soutenir cette décision, tandis que huit pays seulement s’y sont opposés.

**Impact** – Ce vote montre un fort soutien politique mondial pour tenir les pays responsables légalement du changement climatique. C’est un rare moment d’unité après des années de désaccords, notamment avec des pays comme les États-Unis. La résolution encourage les nations à limiter le réchauffement à 1,5 degré Celsius et à aider ceux affectés par le changement climatique. Des groupes autochtones, comme les Māori en Nouvelle-Zélande, considèrent cela comme un grand pas vers la reconnaissance du changement climatique comme un enjeu juridique, pas seulement environnemental.

**Prochaines étapes** – Le secrétaire général de l’ONU fera un rapport l’an prochain sur la façon dont les pays peuvent appliquer les recommandations de la CIJ. La résolution, bien que non contraignante, vise à pousser les gouvernements à agir davantage sur le climat et à rendre des comptes pour les dommages causés par leur inaction.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, la majorité des pays à l’ONU ont reconnu que le changement climatique est une responsabilité légale et que les victimes méritent justice et soutien.
articles.personality_title_es: Las Naciones Unidas respaldan una histórica sentencia sobre la responsabilidad climática de los países
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hace unos seis años, estudiantes de derecho de la Universidad del Pacífico Sur ayudaron a la nación insular de Vanuatu a llevar un caso sobre el cambio climático ante la Corte Internacional de Justicia (CIJ). Argumentaron que el cambio climático es un problema de derechos humanos y que los países deben proteger el planeta para las futuras generaciones.

**Qué pasó** – En 2025, la CIJ emitió una opinión consultiva unánime que dice que la falta de acción de los países frente al cambio climático es un acto incorrecto y que quienes resulten afectados pueden pedir reparaciones. Recientemente, más de 140 países en las Naciones Unidas votaron para apoyar esta decisión, mientras que solo ocho países se opusieron.

**Impacto** – Esta votación muestra un fuerte apoyo político mundial para responsabilizar legalmente a los países por el cambio climático. Representa un raro momento de unidad tras años de desacuerdos, especialmente con países como Estados Unidos. La resolución anima a las naciones a limitar el calentamiento global a 1.5 grados Celsius y a ayudar a quienes sufren por el cambio climático. Grupos indígenas como los Māori en Nueva Zelanda ven esto como un paso importante para reconocer el cambio climático como un asunto legal, no solo ambiental.

**Próximo paso** – El secretario general de la ONU presentará un informe el próximo año sobre cómo los países pueden cumplir con las recomendaciones de la CIJ. La resolución, aunque no es vinculante, busca impulsar a los gobiernos a tomar acciones climáticas más fuertes y rendir cuentas por los daños causados por la inacción.

**Frase clave** – Por primera vez, la mayoría de los países en las Naciones Unidas han acordado que el cambio climático es una responsabilidad legal y que quienes sufren daños merecen justicia y apoyo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting showing a small Pacific island with law books and scales of justice gently resting on its shore, surrounded by a diverse, united circle of glowing, interconnected hands rising from the ocean waves, symbolizing global countries coming together to uphold climate justice and protect vulnerable island nations.

articles.title: Ancient chemistry trick unlocks new type of glass that traps CO2 and hydrogen
articles.summary: Researchers have discovered how to fine-tune a futuristic type of porous glass that can trap gases like CO2 and hydrogen. Inspired by centuries-old glassmaking techniques, the team added sodium and lithium compounds to make the material easier to process and shape. The breakthrough could accelerate the development of high-performance materials for clean energy, gas storage, and advanced manufacturing.
articles.content:
Scientists have adapted a chemistry technique used in traditional glassmaking to improve a futuristic material known as metal-organic framework (MOF) glass. These materials are made from metal atoms connected by organic molecules and are valued for their ability to trap gases such as carbon dioxide and hydrogen, and even capture water.

The international research team, which included scientists from TU Dortmund and the University of Birmingham, reported the findings in Nature Chemistry on May 4. Their work shows that MOF glasses can be adjusted and engineered using methods similar to those long used for conventional glass.

Researchers found that introducing small chemical compounds containing sodium or lithium changes both the structure and behavior of the material. The additives lower the temperature at which the glass softens and make it flow more easily when heated, which could simplify manufacturing.

The discovery creates a new framework for designing customized MOF glasses for advanced technologies. Potential applications include gas separation, chemical storage, advanced coatings, and clean energy systems.

Dr. Dominik Kubicki from the University of Birmingham said: "Glass has been part of human civilization for millennia. From ancient Mesopotamia to modern fiber-optic cables, small amounts of chemical modifiers make it easier to process glass and change its functional properties.

"However, MOF glasses soften only at high temperatures -- above 300 °C -- close to their degradation temperature, making manufacturing challenging and limiting broader use. This discovery unlocks new possibilities for future high-performance materials."

Sodium Changes the Structure of MOF Glass

One of the best-known MOF glasses is ZIF-62, a porous material that can be melted and cooled into a glass while still keeping some of its internal pores. Those pores make it useful for applications such as gas separation, membranes, and catalysis.

Professor Sebastian Henke from TU Dortmund University explained: "Our approach is inspired by how conventional silicate glasses have been modified: disrupting the network structure to tune melting behavior and mechanical properties.

"Our study shows the same principle can be transferred to hybrid metal-organic glasses. This advance brings MOF glasses a step closer to real-world manufacturing and applications in gas separation, storage, catalysis and beyond."

To understand exactly how the sodium additives altered the material, researchers used advanced analysis techniques. Scientists at the University of Birmingham, led by Drs. Dominik Kubicki and Benjamin Gallant, carried out atomic-level studies of the modified glass structure and conducted high-temperature solid-state Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy experiments at the UK High-Field Solid-State NMR Facility.

Their work revealed how sodium ions become integrated into the glass network and weaken some of the connections inside the structure.

AI Modeling Reveals Atomic-Level Changes

Another Birmingham team, led by Professor Andrew Morris and Dr. Mario Ongkiko, used AI-driven computational modeling to help interpret the complex NMR data. Machine-learning-assisted simulations showed how sodium interacted with the glass at the atomic level, confirming the experimental results.

The combined experimental and computational findings showed that sodium does more than simply occupy empty spaces inside the material. Instead, some sodium atoms replace zinc atoms, slightly loosening the glass structure and changing its properties.

Now that scientists better understand how to modify these materials, researchers say additional work is needed to improve their stability, predict their behavior more accurately, and evaluate their performance in real-world technologies.

The study involved researchers from Technische Universität Dortmund, the University of Birmingham, Ruhr-University Bochum, SRM University-AP, the Technical University of Munich, and the University of Cambridge.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in developing a new type of porous glass that can trap CO2 and hydrogen, which has broad implications for clean energy, gas storage, and environmental technologies. The discovery builds on ancient glassmaking techniques to enable easier manufacturing of metal-organic framework glasses, potentially accelerating their real-world applications in reducing greenhouse gases and advancing clean energy solutions. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a tangible advancement with meaningful environmental impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists improve special glass to trap carbon dioxide and hydrogen more easily
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been working on a new kind of glass called metal-organic framework (MOF) glass. This glass is special because it can trap gases like carbon dioxide (CO2) and hydrogen. These gases are important for clean energy and reducing pollution, but making MOF glass has been hard because it needs very high temperatures to shape.

**What happened** – A team of researchers from several universities, including TU Dortmund and the University of Birmingham, found a way to make MOF glass easier to process. They added small amounts of sodium and lithium compounds to the glass. This change lowers the temperature needed to soften the glass and helps it flow better when heated. They used advanced tools like Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) and computer models powered by artificial intelligence (AI) to understand exactly how these additives change the glass’s structure.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it means MOF glass can be made more easily and shaped into useful forms. Since MOF glass can trap CO2 and hydrogen, it could be used in technologies that store gases, separate chemicals, or help clean energy systems work better. By making the glass easier to produce, the research brings these ideas closer to real-life use.

**What's next step** – The scientists want to keep improving the glass by making it stronger and more stable. They also plan to test how well the new glass works in real machines and devices. This future work will help decide how the glass can be used in industries like energy, environment, and manufacturing.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Adding sodium and lithium to MOF glass makes it easier to shape and use, opening new possibilities for trapping gases that help clean energy and reduce pollution.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques améliorent un verre spécial pour mieux piéger le dioxyde de carbone et l’hydrogène
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des scientifiques travaillent sur un nouveau type de verre appelé verre à cadre métal-organique (MOF). Ce verre est spécial car il peut piéger des gaz comme le dioxyde de carbone (CO2) et l’hydrogène. Ces gaz sont importants pour l’énergie propre et la réduction de la pollution, mais fabriquer ce verre est difficile car il faut des températures très élevées pour le modeler.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs de plusieurs universités, dont TU Dortmund et l’Université de Birmingham, a trouvé un moyen de rendre le verre MOF plus facile à travailler. Ils ont ajouté de petites quantités de composés de sodium et de lithium au verre. Ce changement abaisse la température nécessaire pour ramollir le verre et facilite son écoulement à la chaleur. Ils ont utilisé des outils avancés comme la résonance magnétique nucléaire (RMN) et des modèles informatiques utilisant l’intelligence artificielle (IA) pour comprendre comment ces additifs modifient la structure du verre.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle permet de fabriquer le verre MOF plus facilement et de le façonner en formes utiles. Comme ce verre peut piéger le CO2 et l’hydrogène, il pourrait être utilisé dans des technologies de stockage de gaz, de séparation chimique ou pour améliorer les systèmes d’énergie propre. En facilitant sa production, cette recherche rapproche ces idées d’une utilisation réelle.

**Étape suivante** – Les scientifiques veulent continuer à améliorer le verre en le rendant plus solide et stable. Ils prévoient aussi de tester ses performances dans de vrais appareils. Ces travaux futurs aideront à décider comment utiliser ce verre dans l’industrie de l’énergie, de l’environnement et de la fabrication.

**Phrase clé** – Ajouter du sodium et du lithium au verre MOF facilite sa mise en forme et ouvre de nouvelles possibilités pour piéger des gaz aidant à l’énergie propre et à la réduction de la pollution.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos mejoran un vidrio especial para atrapar mejor el dióxido de carbono y el hidrógeno
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos trabajan en un nuevo tipo de vidrio llamado vidrio de estructura metal-orgánica (MOF). Este vidrio es especial porque puede atrapar gases como el dióxido de carbono (CO2) y el hidrógeno. Estos gases son importantes para la energía limpia y reducir la contaminación, pero fabricar este vidrio es difícil porque necesita temperaturas muy altas para moldearlo.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de investigadores de varias universidades, incluyendo TU Dortmund y la Universidad de Birmingham, encontró una manera de hacer que el vidrio MOF sea más fácil de procesar. Añadieron pequeñas cantidades de compuestos de sodio y litio al vidrio. Este cambio baja la temperatura necesaria para ablandar el vidrio y ayuda a que fluya mejor cuando se calienta. Usaron herramientas avanzadas como Resonancia Magnética Nuclear (RMN) y modelos computacionales con inteligencia artificial (IA) para entender cómo estos aditivos cambian la estructura del vidrio.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque permite fabricar el vidrio MOF más fácilmente y darle formas útiles. Como este vidrio puede atrapar CO2 e hidrógeno, podría usarse en tecnologías para almacenar gases, separar químicos o mejorar sistemas de energía limpia. Al facilitar su producción, la investigación acerca estas ideas a un uso real.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren seguir mejorando el vidrio para hacerlo más fuerte y estable. También planean probar cómo funciona el nuevo vidrio en máquinas y dispositivos reales. Este trabajo futuro ayudará a decidir cómo usar el vidrio en industrias como la energía, el medio ambiente y la fabricación.

**Frase clave** – Añadir sodio y litio al vidrio MOF facilita su moldeado y abre nuevas posibilidades para atrapar gases que ayudan a la energía limpia y a reducir la contaminación.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a translucent, intricate glass structure symbolizing metal-organic framework (MOF) glass, with glowing sodium and lithium ions gently weaving into its lattice like shimmering threads; surrounding the glass are stylized, softly glowing bubbles representing trapped carbon dioxide and hydrogen molecules, all set against a simple, natural background of soft earth and sky hues conveying innovation and harmony.

articles.title: Amsterdam strips meat and fossil fuel ads from its public spaces
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Since May 1, Amsterdam’s billboards and tram shelters no longer carry ads for burgers, petrol cars, or cheap flights. The Dutch capital is now the first in the world to ban public advertising for both meat and fossil fuel products. Where chicken nuggets and SUVs once competed for wall […]
The post Amsterdam strips meat and fossil fuel ads from its public spaces first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Since May 1, Amsterdam’s billboards and tram shelters no longer carry ads for burgers, petrol cars, or cheap flights. The Dutch capital is now the first in the world to ban public advertising for both meat and fossil fuel products.

Where chicken nuggets and SUVs once competed for wall space, the Rijksmuseum and a piano concert now fill the gaps.

The targets behind the ban: carbon neutral by 2050, and meat consumption halved over the same period. Councillors from the GreenLeft Party and Party for the Animals co-authored the restrictions, dismissing industry objections as self-interested.

“The climate crisis is very urgent,” says Anneke Veenhoff from the GreenLeft Party. “If you want to be leading in climate policies and you rent out your walls to exactly the opposite, then what are you doing?”

Grouping meat with fossil fuels is the point

Meat made up an estimated 0.1 percent of Amsterdam’s outdoor ad market before the ban. Politically, that’s not the point. Placing burgers alongside flights and diesel cars in the same restricted category reframes dietary choice as a climate issue. That’s the political argument, and it’s deliberate.

Anke Bakker, Amsterdam’s group leader for Party for the Animals and the politician who drove the ban, pushes back on nanny-state accusations. “We are trying to get the big companies not to tell us all the time what we need to eat and buy,” she says. “In a way, we’re giving people more freedom because they can make their own choice.”

The Dutch Meat Association has called the move “an undesirable way to influence consumer behaviour,” adding that meat “delivers essential nutrients and should remain visible and accessible to consumers.” The Dutch Association of Travel Agents and Tour Operators described it as a disproportionate curb on commercial freedom.

Amsterdam is not the first, but it’s the most visible

Haarlem, 18 kilometers (11 miles) to the west, announced a broad ban on most meat advertising in 2022, which came into force in 2024 alongside a fossil fuel ad ban. Utrecht and Nijmegen followed. Edinburgh, Sheffield, Stockholm, and Florence have moved to restrict fossil fuel advertising. France has a nationwide ban.

When a capital city moves, other governments notice.

Activists like lawyer Hannah Prins, who worked with campaign group Fossil-Free Advertising to push the ban through, are framing it as a “tobacco moment” for high-carbon food. “Johan Cruyff would be in advertisements for tobacco,” Prins says. “That used to be normal. He died of lung cancer. What we see in our public space is what we find normal in our society.”

Does removing ads actually change behaviour?

Researchers don’t yet know. There’s no direct evidence that stripping meat ads from bus shelters moves people toward plant-based diets.

Prof Joreintje Mackenbach, an epidemiologist from Amsterdam University Medical Center, thinks Amsterdam is running a useful natural experiment. “If we see advertisements for fast food everywhere, it normalizes the consumption of fast consumption,” she says. “So if we take away those types of cues in our public living environments, then that is also going to have an impact on those social norms.”

Worth noting: a study of London Underground’s 2019 ban on junk food advertising found fewer people buying those products across the UK capital.

The bigger problem the ban doesn’t solve is digital. The same 19-euro ($21.00) flight to Berlin that disappeared from the tram shelter keeps showing up in social media feeds. Prins knows the restriction doesn’t reach that far. For her, that’s beside the point. Cities decide what their walls say. Amsterdam finally said something.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: Amsterdam has implemented a pioneering and significant public policy banning advertisements for meat and fossil fuel products in public spaces, aiming to reduce carbon emissions and meat consumption by 2050. This action has broad societal and environmental impact, reframes dietary choices as climate issues, and sets a precedent for other cities and countries. The article provides detailed context on the policy, political motivations, and potential behavioral impacts, making it a substantive and inspiring environmental story.
articles.personality_title: Amsterdam bans meat and fossil fuel ads in public spaces to fight climate change
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Amsterdam, the capital of the Netherlands, has set goals to become carbon neutral by 2050 and reduce meat consumption by half. Advertising for meat and fossil fuels was common in the city’s public areas, promoting burgers, petrol cars, and cheap flights.

**What happened** – Starting May 1, Amsterdam stopped showing ads for meat products and fossil fuels on billboards, tram shelters, and other public spaces. Instead of ads, these spaces now display cultural content like museum promotions and piano concerts. This makes Amsterdam the first city in the world to ban both meat and fossil fuel advertising in public.

**Impact** – The ban links eating meat and using fossil fuels directly to climate change, encouraging people to think about their choices differently. Although meat ads were only a small part of public advertising, this policy challenges companies that push these products. It also gives people a chance to decide without constant advertising pressure. Other cities in the Netherlands and Europe have similar bans, but Amsterdam’s high visibility may inspire more places to follow.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will watch how this ban affects people’s habits over time. While ads on social media and digital platforms continue, Amsterdam’s move shows that cities can control public messages and influence social norms. This could lead to more cities adopting similar policies and expanding restrictions beyond traditional advertising.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Amsterdam’s removal of meat and fossil fuel ads from public spaces marks a new approach to addressing climate change by changing what people see every day.
articles.personality_title_fr: Amsterdam interdit les publicités pour la viande et les combustibles fossiles dans les espaces publics
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Amsterdam, capitale des Pays-Bas, vise à devenir neutre en carbone d’ici 2050 et à réduire de moitié la consommation de viande. La publicité pour la viande et les combustibles fossiles était courante dans les espaces publics, promouvant burgers, voitures à essence et vols pas chers.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Depuis le 1er mai, Amsterdam a interdit les publicités pour la viande et les combustibles fossiles sur les panneaux d’affichage, abris de tramway et autres lieux publics. Ces espaces affichent désormais des contenus culturels comme des promotions de musées ou des concerts de piano. Amsterdam est la première ville au monde à bannir ces deux types de publicités.

**Impact** – Cette interdiction relie directement la consommation de viande et l’utilisation des combustibles fossiles au changement climatique, invitant les habitants à repenser leurs choix. Bien que les pubs pour la viande représentaient une petite partie des annonces, cette politique défie les entreprises qui poussent ces produits et permet aux gens de choisir sans pression publicitaire constante. D’autres villes aux Pays-Bas et en Europe ont des interdictions similaires, mais la visibilité d’Amsterdam pourrait en inspirer d’autres.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs observeront l’impact de cette interdiction sur les habitudes des citoyens. Même si la publicité numérique continue, la décision d’Amsterdam montre que les villes peuvent contrôler les messages publics et influencer les normes sociales. Cela pourrait encourager plus de villes à adopter des mesures similaires et à étendre ces restrictions.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En retirant les publicités pour la viande et les combustibles fossiles, Amsterdam adopte une nouvelle stratégie pour lutter contre le changement climatique en modifiant ce que les gens voient chaque jour.
articles.personality_title_es: Ámsterdam prohíbe anuncios de carne y combustibles fósiles en espacios públicos para combatir el cambio climático
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Ámsterdam, la capital de los Países Bajos, tiene como objetivo ser neutral en carbono para 2050 y reducir a la mitad el consumo de carne. Anuncios de carne y combustibles fósiles eran comunes en espacios públicos, promoviendo hamburguesas, autos de gasolina y vuelos baratos.

**Qué pasó** – Desde el 1 de mayo, Ámsterdam dejó de mostrar anuncios de productos cárnicos y combustibles fósiles en carteles, paradas de tranvía y otros espacios públicos. En lugar de anuncios, ahora se muestran contenidos culturales como promociones de museos y conciertos de piano. Ámsterdam es la primera ciudad en el mundo en prohibir ambos tipos de publicidad en espacios públicos.

**Impacto** – La prohibición relaciona directamente la carne y los combustibles fósiles con el cambio climático, invitando a las personas a pensar diferente sobre sus elecciones. Aunque los anuncios de carne eran una pequeña parte del mercado publicitario, esta medida desafía a las empresas que los promueven y ofrece a las personas más libertad para decidir sin presión constante. Otras ciudades en los Países Bajos y Europa tienen prohibiciones similares, pero la visibilidad de Ámsterdam puede inspirar a más lugares.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores observarán cómo esta prohibición afecta los hábitos de las personas con el tiempo. Aunque la publicidad digital sigue presente, la acción de Ámsterdam muestra que las ciudades pueden controlar los mensajes públicos y cambiar normas sociales. Esto podría llevar a que más ciudades adopten políticas parecidas y amplíen las restricciones.

**Resumen en una frase** – La eliminación de anuncios de carne y combustibles fósiles en Ámsterdam representa un nuevo enfoque para enfrentar el cambio climático cambiando lo que la gente ve todos los días.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant Amsterdam street scene where colorful tram shelters and billboards are filled with peaceful images of a grand piano concert and the iconic Rijksmuseum, while symbolic silhouettes of a crossed-out burger and a fading fossil fuel pump subtly blend into the background, all rendered in soft, natural tones to evoke a hopeful shift toward sustainability and mindful choices.

articles.title: Rosa Pachurí Paraba: How Indigenous women are leading Bolivia’s response to wildfires
articles.summary: Bolivia’s 2024 wildfires devastated Santa Cruz, but Indigenous Chiquitano and Monkox communities led recovery through collective organization, ancestral knowledge and fire management strategies, and revived food systems amid delayed state response.
articles.content:
By Mary Luz Guzman Ruiz

This article by Muy Waso was first published on April 7, 2026, under the program Get Ready for the COP. An edited version is being republished on Global Voices under a content partnership agreement.

The story is part of Global Voices’ May 2026 Spotlight, “Global crisis, local solutions.” This series offers stories of resistance and successful climate action, insight into how communities in the Global South are fighting back against the crisis, analysis of what this might mean for future generations, and more. You can support this coverage by donating here.

In 2024, Bolivia experienced the worst forest fire catastrophe in its history: 12.6 million hectares burned, more than double the amount in 2019. In Chiquitanía, one of the hardest-hit regions, Indigenous communities lost forests, crops, and water sources. In the Josema community, in the municipality of San Rafael de Velasco, Rosa Pachurí Paraba, president of the Regional Organization of Chiquitana Indigenous Women (ORMICH), is fighting to recover what the fire took away: her people’s food sovereignty.

When she was a little girl, Pachurí Paraba could still enjoy a delicious cassava soup accompanied by fresh fish caught from the river’ those ingredients were enough for her mother to feed the family. Today, she can no longer eat the dish she loved so much because of the fires and the drought — and her story is not an exception.

According to the Ombudsman’s Office of Bolivia, forest fires and uncontrolled burning devastated 12,658,157 hectares in 2024, a figure that exceeds all historical records in the country. The most affected department was Santa Cruz, with 8.5 million hectares burned (68 percent of the department's total), according to Fundación Tierra’s 2024 report on the fires.

To put the catastrophe into perspective, it is as if a territory equivalent to the size of Cuba had burned. The Foundation for the Conservation of the Chiquitano Forest (FCBC) calls the Chiquitano Dry Forest the largest and best-preserved tropical dry forest in South America.

When the rains stop, the forest becomes extremely fragile: if fire is used improperly, it becomes a devastating threat. According to an analysis by the FCBC on the country’s 2019 fires, at ground level the humus layer is lost, invertebrates and fungi essential for decomposing organic matter are eliminated, and minerals and nutrients are volatilized, making the soil less fertile. The seed bank that allows the natural regeneration of the forest is almost completely lost.

In 2024, Bolivia experienced the worst wildfire season in its history. It was a very painful season for communities located in the department of Santa Cruz; by September, the government declared a national disaster.

In the municipality of San Rafael de Velasco, the fires burned more than 201,000 hectares, equivalent to 20.9 percent of its territory. The mayor of San Rafael, Jorge Vargas Roca, publicly cried out of helplessness in the face of the devastation, while denouncing that there was only a handful of volunteer firefighters on hand to confront half a million hectares burning across the Chiquitania region.

In Josema, where Pachurí Paraba lives, they only experienced smoke, but neighboring communities shared devastating accounts of families who lost their homes and their crops. The impact on food security, however, was regional.

Leadership born from the ashes

As president of ORMICH — an organization that, according to Cultural Survival, brings together 800 women from five provinces in the department of Santa Cruz — Pachurí Paraba assisted people both during and after the emergency, by providing them with shelter and other resources.

The Casa Grande, from which women obtain materials to market handicrafts and cosmetic products, has emerged as a key economic resource. Pachurí Paraba’s testimony to Cultural Survival reveals that speaking about this is still difficult. The fire not only took her forest; in some cases, families even lost their homes.

Elizabeth Arteaga, who hails from another Chiquitano community, lost her family’s plantain, cassava, and corn crops. Her father-in-law, whose lungs could not withstand the smoke, also did not survive. Those affected by these fires carry a trauma they learn to live with, but from which they never fully recover.

In the midst of this tragedy, the state response was lacking. Despite demands from local authorities, Indigenous communities, and civil society, the government took weeks to declare a national disaster.

Faced with the state void, the response came from the collective. Communities organized themselves, formed environmental monitors to stay constantly alert to any heat spots, and women took on an unprecedented leading role.

The IOM documents that, with support from international organizations, Chiquitano communities now have evacuation plans and trained brigades. According to the same report, this was the first time resources were allocated based on a forecast that anticipated unusually intense fires.

As the land has not yet fully recovered and the climate is uncertain, harvests are not enough to provide for everyone equally. Faced with this situation, communities have revived barter, an ancestral practice that today functions as their main food security safety net. If a family only managed to harvest corn, it seeks another that had success with peanuts or rice; thus, through fair exchange, tables are filled again.

In Chiquitanía, food security is built not only with external assistance, but also sustained in memory. After the devastating fires, some communities have come to understand that their greatest emergency reserve lies in the knowledge of their elders.

A study published in 2023 in the Environmental Science and Policy journal documented how Bolivia’s Monkox people developed a controlled burning protocol, a fire monitoring program, watershed and forest conservation policies, and cultural revitalization strategies — all with ancestral knowledge as the central axis, and Western technical knowledge as a complement.

The study explains that fire is the main tool of Monkox agriculture, an ancestral method passed down from generation to generation that helps fertilize and aerate the soil. Under the approach of the Indigenous Confederation of Native Communities of Lomerío (CICOL), Indigenous knowledge is placed at the center of fire management strategy.

For communities affected by fire, recovery comes from two fronts: evidence-based public policies and a return to ancestral wisdom. Fundación Alternativas, which specializes in food security and climate change in Bolivia, has proposed strategies that include promoting diversified production in polycultures to reduce the use of agrochemicals, providing technical assistance to small producers, promoting sustainable irrigation methods, and strengthening local food processing enterprises.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on Indigenous communities in Bolivia leading effective wildfire recovery and fire management using ancestral knowledge and collective organization, which has broad environmental and social significance. It highlights a meaningful, large-scale response to a devastating climate-related disaster, with clear details on community leadership, food security revival, and integration of traditional and scientific fire management strategies.
articles.personality_title: Indigenous women lead Bolivia’s fight to recover from historic wildfires
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
In 2024, Bolivia faced its worst wildfire season ever, burning more than 12 million hectares, especially in the Santa Cruz region. These fires destroyed forests, crops, and water sources, deeply affecting Indigenous communities like the Chiquitano and Monkox.

**What happened**
Rosa Pachurí Paraba, leader of the Chiquitana Indigenous Women’s group ORMICH, helped her community respond to the disaster. While the government was slow to act, Indigenous groups organized themselves, created fire-monitoring teams, and revived food-sharing traditions to support families who lost crops and homes. They combined their ancestral fire management knowledge with new techniques to better protect the land.

**Impact**
This response shows how Indigenous communities can lead effective recovery after climate disasters. Their use of traditional fire methods helps keep forests healthy and soil fertile, while their collective efforts restored food security through barter systems. This approach blends old wisdom with modern science, offering a strong model for other regions facing wildfires.

**What's next step**
Communities and experts are working to develop public policies that support sustainable farming, fire control, and local food production. Continued training, better resources, and respect for Indigenous knowledge will be key to preventing future disasters and helping the land fully recover.

**One-sentence takeaway**
Indigenous women in Bolivia are leading a powerful recovery from devastating wildfires by combining ancestral knowledge and community action to restore forests and food security.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les femmes autochtones mènent la lutte en Bolivie pour se remettre des incendies historiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
En 2024, la Bolivie a vécu sa pire saison d’incendies jamais enregistrée, avec plus de 12 millions d’hectares brûlés, notamment dans la région de Santa Cruz. Ces feux ont détruit forêts, cultures et sources d’eau, touchant profondément les communautés autochtones comme les Chiquitano et Monkox.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
Rosa Pachurí Paraba, présidente de l’organisation des femmes autochtones chiquitanas (ORMICH), a aidé sa communauté à répondre à la catastrophe. Face à la lenteur de l’État, les groupes autochtones se sont organisés, ont créé des équipes de surveillance des feux et ont relancé des traditions d’échange alimentaire pour soutenir les familles ayant perdu leurs récoltes et leurs maisons. Ils ont associé leurs connaissances ancestrales de gestion du feu à de nouvelles techniques pour mieux protéger la terre.

**Impact**
Cette réponse montre comment les communautés autochtones peuvent diriger une récupération efficace après des catastrophes climatiques. Leur utilisation des méthodes traditionnelles de feu aide à maintenir les forêts en bonne santé et le sol fertile, tandis que leurs efforts collectifs ont restauré la sécurité alimentaire grâce à des systèmes de troc. Cette approche mêle sagesse ancienne et science moderne, offrant un modèle solide pour d’autres régions confrontées aux incendies.

**Prochaine étape**
Les communautés et les experts travaillent à développer des politiques publiques soutenant l’agriculture durable, le contrôle du feu et la production alimentaire locale. La formation continue, de meilleures ressources et le respect des savoirs autochtones seront essentiels pour prévenir de futures catastrophes et aider la terre à se rétablir complètement.

**Résumé en une phrase**
Les femmes autochtones de Bolivie mènent une puissante reconstruction après des incendies dévastateurs en combinant savoirs ancestraux et action communautaire pour restaurer forêts et sécurité alimentaire.
articles.personality_title_es: Mujeres indígenas lideran la recuperación de Bolivia tras incendios históricos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
En 2024, Bolivia sufrió su peor temporada de incendios forestales, con más de 12 millones de hectáreas quemadas, especialmente en la región de Santa Cruz. Estos incendios destruyeron bosques, cultivos y fuentes de agua, afectando profundamente a comunidades indígenas como los Chiquitano y Monkox.

**Qué pasó**
Rosa Pachurí Paraba, líder del grupo de Mujeres Indígenas Chiquitanas (ORMICH), ayudó a su comunidad a responder ante el desastre. Mientras el gobierno actuaba lentamente, los grupos indígenas se organizaron, crearon equipos para monitorear incendios y revivieron tradiciones de intercambio de alimentos para apoyar a las familias que perdieron cultivos y hogares. Combinaron sus conocimientos ancestrales sobre el manejo del fuego con nuevas técnicas para proteger mejor la tierra.

**Impacto**
Esta respuesta muestra cómo las comunidades indígenas pueden liderar una recuperación efectiva tras desastres climáticos. El uso de métodos tradicionales de fuego ayuda a mantener los bosques saludables y el suelo fértil, mientras que sus esfuerzos colectivos restauraron la seguridad alimentaria mediante sistemas de trueque. Esta combinación de sabiduría antigua y ciencia moderna ofrece un modelo fuerte para otras regiones con incendios.

**Próximo paso**
Comunidades y expertos trabajan para desarrollar políticas públicas que apoyen la agricultura sostenible, el control del fuego y la producción local de alimentos. La capacitación continua, mejores recursos y respeto por el conocimiento indígena serán claves para prevenir futuros desastres y ayudar a la tierra a recuperarse completamente.

**Conclusión en una frase**
Las mujeres indígenas en Bolivia lideran una poderosa recuperación tras incendios devastadores al combinar conocimiento ancestral y acción comunitaria para restaurar bosques y seguridad alimentaria.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vibrant tropical dry forest regenerating after wildfire, with symbolic silhouettes of Indigenous women weaving baskets and exchanging crops in a circle, surrounded by stylized flames transforming into flourishing cassava plants and rivers flowing anew, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and muted greens.
